nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef slopes and lagoons, typically on rock, rubble, and lower reef zones where light is reduced and nutrient levels are moderate.

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Benefits from supplemental feeding of small meaty foods such as brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, or nanoplankton approximately once per week. Spot feeding by pipette can improve growth and coloration. Overfeeding can degrade water quality.

goodWith: other mushroom corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals placed in direct contact (torch, hammer, frogspawn, chalice)
large urchins that may dislodge frags

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by RevolverOcelot; 2560x1920. Sharp aquarium shot under bluish reef lighting showing the whole disc densely covered in the finger-like 'hairy' projections diagnostic of Rhodactis indosinensis; most striking of the pool.

Photo by Philippe Bourjon; 1200x888. In-situ reef shot, green disc with dense brown hairy tentacles clearly showing the furry surface texture; colorful. Uploader flagged ID 'to confirm' but morphology matches.
Photo by Chaloklum Diving; 892x750. In-situ top-down view of the whole disc undergoing fission, showing the hairy finger-like projections; lower resolution and duller brown coloration, kept as extra.

summary: The Hairy Mushroom Coral (Rhodactis indosinensis) is a reef animal closely related to stony corals but lacking a hard skeleton, easily recognized by the dense, finger-like projections that cover its disc and give it a furry appearance. It is widely considered one of the more forgiving corals for beginners and generally does well under low flow and low-to-moderate lighting.
careNotes:
The Hairy Mushroom Coral is distinguished from other mushroom corals by its densely textured disc surface, which is covered in short, finger-like projections that give the animal a furry or hairy appearance. Although sold and commonly described as a coral, it belongs to the order Corallimorpharia — a group related to true stony corals but lacking a calcium carbonate skeleton, meaning it produces no hard structure at all.

Lighting: Low to moderate light is generally advised, targeting approximately 40–150 PAR. If the disc remains shrunken or pale during the first week after introduction, light intensity may be too high; move the specimen to a lower position in the tank and acclimate it gradually before increasing exposure.

Flow: Low flow is strongly preferred. Direct current from a powerhead has been known to cause tissue stress, shrinking, and detachment. Indirect or gently alternating flow is ideal.

Placement: Lower tank zones are recommended, where light and flow are gentler and water conditions are more stable.

Aggression: The Hairy Mushroom Coral is generally considered semi-aggressive. It has been known to sting and overgrow neighboring corals when in direct contact, so adequate spacing from other LPS corals and softies is important. In tanks with large, dense mushroom colonies, chemical compounds released into the water (a process called allelopathy) can affect nearby corals; running activated carbon is sometimes recommended in high-density mushroom setups.

Toxicity/Handling: Like all corallimorpharians, this species possesses nematocysts (stinging cells). Gloves are recommended when handling, and hands should be washed thoroughly after any contact.

Predatory behavior: The Hairy Mushroom Coral can expand and close its disc to engulf small invertebrates and food items — a more pronounced predatory behavior than is typical of gentler Discosoma mushrooms. It generally does not pose a threat to fish or most invertebrates under normal aquarium conditions.

Reproduction: Reproduces asexually via longitudinal fission (splitting down the center) and pedal laceration (small tissue pieces left behind as the animal moves). Propagation by cutting mature specimens is practiced in the hobby.

Host behavior: Clownfish have been documented using large Hairy Mushroom colonies as host anemone substitutes.

Calcium and alkalinity demands are lower than those of stony corals because no skeletal deposition occurs. Maintaining stable parameters matters more than chasing precision highs.
breedingNotes: Reproduces asexually in captivity via longitudinal fission and pedal laceration. Fragging by cutting mature specimens in half is a commonly practiced propagation method. Sexual reproduction occurs on natural reefs but is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: ReefChasers – Hairy Mushroom Coral Care Guide
primary.notes: Primary care guide for Rhodactis indosinensis specifically. Provided temperature, pH, alkalinity, calcium, PAR range (75–150), flow (low-moderate), care level (beginner), and adult size.

additional.name: AquariumDomain – Hairy Mushroom Coral Species Profile
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 72–78°F (22–26°C), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12. Flow: moderate with alternating/indirect current preference. Confirmed semi-aggressive temperament and Indo-Pacific origin. Family listed as Discosomatidae.

additional.name: Extreme Corals – Rhodactis Mushroom Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 76–80°F, salinity 1.024–1.026, pH 8.0–8.4, alkalinity 7–10 dKH. PAR 75–150. Confirmed beginner-to-intermediate care level and supplemental feeding 1–2x/week.

additional.name: TankFacts – Hairy Mushroom Rhodactis
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 72–78°F (22–26°C), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.023–1.025, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1200–1350 ppm. Confirmed Family Discosomidae and Indo-Pacific native range.

additional.name: World Register of Marine Species (WoRMS) – Rhodactis indosinensis
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only (AphiaID 290981). Confirmed Order Corallimorpharia, Family Discosomidae, original description by Carlgren 1943. Documented type specimens from Vietnamese and Japanese EEZs.
